title: TOPIK I Authentic Mock Exam 04 — Guided Course

courses.title: Everyday Questions and Replies
objectives: Answer yes/no and wh-questions by echoing the right word
Use set phrases to reply to an apology or thanks

new_grammar.explanation: Polite negation of the copula: denies that something or someone is N.
Polite question with a descriptive verb; answer by confirming or denying the same descriptive verb.
Marks the time when an action happens; required in answers to 언제 questions (except with 오늘/내일/어제).

courses.review: You can answer everyday questions with the matching word and reply to thanks and apologies.

courses.title: Places and Topics
objectives: Identify a place from the words spoken there (bank, pharmacy, hotel)
Name the overall topic of a short exchange

new_grammar.explanation: States a desire while inviting the listener's response; common when making requests at counters.
Expresses frequency per unit of time: 하루에 세 번 = three times a day.
new_grammar.example: 이 약을 하루에 세 번 드세요.

new_grammar.explanation: Attaches to time nouns to mean 'every': 토요일마다, 아침마다, 주말마다.

courses.review: You can tell where a conversation happens and state what it is mainly about.

courses.title: Following Conversations
objectives: Track who does what when a plan changes mid-dialogue
Catch small words (아직, 처음) that decide the right detail

new_grammar.explanation: Expresses what someone intends or is about to do.
Indicates that something (often an accident) happened in the middle of doing an action.
States the purpose of going: V-(으)러 가다 = go in order to V.

courses.review: You can follow a changing plan and pin down the exact detail being asked.

courses.title: Opinions, Announcements, and Reasons
objectives: Find a speaker's main idea, ignoring the other person's habit
Catch the key facts and purpose of a public announcement

new_grammar.explanation: Conditional: if/when A, then B. Used for general advice.
Before doing V: 약을 먹기 전에 = before taking medicine.
new_grammar.example: 약을 먹기 전에 물을 많이 마셔요.

new_grammar.explanation: Concedes one point before asserting the main one: hard, BUT worth it.

courses.review: You can state a speaker's main idea and read off the facts of an announcement.

courses.title: Reading Foundations
objectives: Choose the topic of two short sentences from their predicates
Fill a blank with the right particle, verb, adverb, or conjunction

new_grammar.explanation: Marks where an action verb happens; required with activity verbs like 산책하다, 운동하다, 먹다.
Does an action together with N; 같이 strengthens the companionship reading of 하고.
Expresses obligation: 가야 합니다 = must go.

courses.review: You can pick the topic of a short text and fill blanks with the right small word.

courses.title: Notices and Short Passages
objectives: Check each statement against a notice and find the one that is false
Match a short passage to its detail or its main idea

new_grammar.explanation: Marks the starting point of a range; with ages it sets the minimum.
Deadline before an event: arrive by N before it happens.
new_grammar.example: 출발 한 시간 전까지 공항에 오세요.

new_grammar.explanation: Condition plus ability: if you do A, you can do B - a common main-idea frame.
new_grammar.example: 돈을 조금씩 모으면 사고 싶은 것을 살 수 있습니다.

courses.review: You can spot a false statement in a notice and read a short passage for detail and main idea.

courses.title: Paired Passages I
objectives: Match a verb's tense to past time words in a paired passage
Identify a feeling and the right grammatical form for a blank

new_grammar.explanation: Formal past tense; required with past time expressions like 지난겨울.
Negative command stem: V-지 말다 = do not V; in reported speech, V-지 말라고 하다.
Ability or possibility: V-(으)ㄹ 수 있다 = can V.
new_grammar.example: 시험에 합격하면 파란 띠를 받을 수 있습니다.

courses.review: You can keep tenses straight, read a writer's feeling, and choose the correct verb form.

courses.title: Ordering and Longer Passages
objectives: Order scrambled sentences using time words and connectives
Read a longer passage for its purpose and matching detail

new_grammar.explanation: Introduces the consequence of the previous sentence; in ordering items it must directly follow its cause.
To decide/agree to do; past tense recalls an earlier arrangement.
Or - connects alternative verbs/adjectives: dark or shaken.

courses.review: You can put a story in order and handle a longer passage's purpose and details.
